caption:
7 iterator helpers that just landed in JavaScript.

Lazy .map(), .filter(), .take() — without creating intermediate arrays. Process millions of items with zero memory overhead.

ES2026. All browsers. No polyfills.

📌 SAVE this — you'll be using these daily.

Which one surprised you? 👇

#javascript #es2026 #iterators #webdev #coding #programming #developer #performance #cleancode #codingtips

slides.title: Iterator helpers that just landed in JavaScript
slides.subtitle: Lazy evaluation. Zero intermediate arrays. ES2026.

slides.title: .map( ) — transform lazily
slides.desc: Like Array.map but doesn't create a new array. Transforms on demand.

slides.title: .filter( ) — skip lazily
slides.desc: Filters without creating an intermediate array first.

slides.title: .take( ) — grab the first N
slides.desc: Stop iteration after N items. Like .slice(0, N) but lazy.

slides.title: .drop( ) — skip the first N
slides.desc: The opposite of .take(). Skip N items, keep the rest.

slides.title: .flatMap( ) — flatten + transform
slides.desc: Map and flatten in one pass. Lazy.

slides.title: .reduce( ) — accumulate lazily
slides.desc: Same as Array.reduce but works on any iterator.
slides.punchline: The full pipeline in one chain.

slides.title: .toArray( ) — materialize
slides.desc: Convert the lazy iterator back to an array when you need it.
slides.code:
// Everything before toArray()
// is lazy — no work done yet.

const result = hugeDataset
  .values()
  .filter(predicate)
  .map(transform)
  .take(100)
  .toArray();  // NOW it runs

slides.title: Iterator Helpers Cheat Sheet
slides.question: Which helper are you using first? 👇
